items.foco: Classic triad: areflexia, ascending paralysis, albuminocytological dissociation
items.title: classical triad
items.contentHtml:
Classic triad

  • The triad includes arreflexia (symmetrical loss of deep tendon reflexes), ascending paralysis (weakness that progresses from lower to upper limbs in hours/days) and albuminocytological dissociation in cerebrospinal fluid.
  • albuminocytological dissociation consists of elevated proteins (>45 mg/dL) without pleocytosis (<10 cells/µL); It typically appears from day 7 after the onset of symptoms.
  • Ascending paralysis can involve respiratory and bulbar muscles; The Inverted Lasegue's sign is an early finding.
  • Differential diagnosis: Transverse myelitis (sensory level), myasthenia gravis (fluctuation), botulism (descending paralysis).

items.foco: Most common variant: acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy
items.title: Most common variant (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy)
items.contentHtml:
Most common variant

  • Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy represents >90% of cases in North America and Europe.
  • Pathophysiology: autoimmune attack mediated by T lymphocytes and antibodies against peripheral myelin, with macrophage infiltration and segmental demyelination.
  • Clinic: ascending weakness symmetrical, early areflexia, distal sensory alteration in glove and sock, and lumbar or radicular pain.
  • Electromyography shows conduction block and absent F waves as typical findings of demyelination.
  • Axonal variant: acute motor and sensory axonal neuropathy (sensory-motor axonal) and acute motor axonal neuropathy (pure motor axonal), more common in Asia and associated with Campylobacter jejuni.

items.foco: Infectious history: Campylobacter jejuni, CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma
items.title: Infectious history
items.contentHtml:
Infectious history

  • Two thirds of cases have infection 1-3 weeks prior: Campylobacter jejuni (more common, associated with axonal variant), CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma pneumoniae.
  • Campylobacter jejuni: induces antibodies anti-GM1 by molecular mimicry with bacterial lipopolysaccharides; It is associated with a worse prognosis.
  • CMV and EBV: more linked to acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy; CMV can present anti-GM2 antibodies.
  • Mycoplasma pneumoniae: produces anti-galactocereboside antibodies; also associated with Miller Fisher.
  • Vaccination (influenza, meningococcus) is rarely associated; There is no solid evidence of causality with the COVID-19 vaccine.

items.foco: cerebrospinal fluid finding: elevated protein without pleocytosis
items.title: cerebrospinal fluid Finding
items.contentHtml:
cerebrospinal fluid Finding

  • Albuminocytological dissociation is the elevation of proteins in the cerebrospinal fluid (>45 mg/dL, typically 100-1000 mg/dL) with leukocyte count <10 cells/µL.
  • It appears from day 7; may be absent in the first week (sensitivity ~50% at the beginning, >90% at 14 days).
  • If there is pleocytosis (>50 cells), reconsider diagnosis: human immunodeficiency virus, neurosarcoidosis, infectious meningitis, poliomyelitis.
  • The elevation of proteins is due to root inflammation and alteration of the blood-brain barrier; It does not correlate with severity.
  • Repeat lumbar puncture is not necessary if the symptoms are typical.

items.foco: Electromyography: conduction block, absent F waves
items.title: Electromyography
items.contentHtml:
Electromyography

  • Findings of demyelination: conduction block (>50% drop in compound muscle action potential amplitude between proximal and distal), absent F waves or with prolonged latency, reduced conduction velocity (<80% of the lower limit of normal).
  • In typical acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, conduction block is multifocal; absent F waves indicate early proximal involvement.
  • In the axonal variant (acute motor axonal neuropathy), sensory action potentials are normal, but motor ones show reduced amplitudes without significant block.
  • Needle electromyography can show acute denervation (fibrillations) from the second week onwards.
  • Serial repetition helps predict prognosis: absence of reinnervation at 6 months suggests poor recovery.

items.title: Anti-ganglioside antibodies
items.contentHtml:
Anti-ganglioside antibodies

  • Anti-GM1: associated with axonal motor variant (acute motor axonal neuropathy) and Campylobacter jejuni infection; its presence indicates a worse prognosis and slower recovery.
  • Anti-GQ1b: specific to the Miller Fisher variant (ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, areflexia); also present in Bickerstaff syndrome (stem encephalitis). It is not always necessary for diagnosis if the symptoms are typical.
  • Anti-ganglioside antibodies are not routinely measured in typical acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy; They are reserved for atypical or variant cases.

items.foco: Miller Fisher variant: ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, areflexia
items.title: Miller Fisher variant
items.contentHtml:
Miller Fisher variant

  • Classic triad: ophthalmoplegia (conjugate gaze palsy, ptosis, diplopia), ataxia (trunk and limb weakness, without significant weakness), and generalized arreflexia.
  • Associated with anti-GQ1b (sensitivity >90%) and often preceded by Campylobacter jejuni or Haemophilus influenzae infection.
  • Ophthalmoplegia is symmetrical and may be the first symptom; ataxia is disproportionate to the degree of weakness.
  • Brainstem variant: Bickerstaff syndrome (ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + hyperreflexia or pyramidal signs + altered level of consciousness).
  • cerebrospinal fluid shows albuminocytological dissociation in ~50% of cases; The treatment is the same as in acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy: intravenous immunoglobulin or plasmapheresis.

items.foco: Treatment of choice: intravenous immunoglobulin or plasmapheresis
items.title: Treatment of choice
items.contentHtml:
Treatment of choice

  • First line: intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days (total dose 2 g/kg) or plasmapheresis (5 sessions in 7-14 days, exchanging 1.5 times the plasma volume each session).
  • Both are equally effective; It is not recommended to combine them (it does not show additional benefit and increases costs).
  • IGIV is preferred in children, pregnant women, patients with hemodynamic instability or difficult vascular access.
  • Start treatment as soon as possible, ideally within the first 2 weeks of the onset of symptoms, to limit progression.
  • Contraindication of corticosteroids: they have no benefit and can worsen the prognosis; They are not used as monotherapy or adjuvant.

items.foco: Indication for plasmapheresis: rapid progression, respiratory failure
items.title: Indication for plasmapheresis
items.contentHtml:
Indication for plasmapheresis

  • Plasmapheresis is indicated in cases of rapid progression (weakness that reaches the trunk in <48 h) or imminent respiratory failure (forced vital capacity <20 mL/kg or need for mechanical ventilation).
  • It is also considered if there is a contraindication for IVIG (for example hyperviscosity, severe renal failure, history of thrombosis, IgA deficiency with anti-IgA antibodies).
  • Plasmapheresis eliminates pathogenic antibodies and inflammatory factors; It should be performed in centers with experience.
  • Adverse effects: hypotension, hypocalcemia (due to citrate), catheter infection, allergic reactions.
  • Not recommended in patients with severe hemodynamic instability or active sepsis.

items.foco: intensive care unit admission criteria: forced vital capacity less than 20 mL/kg
items.title: intensive care unit admission criteria
items.contentHtml:
intensive care unit admission criteria

  • The main criterion is forced vital capacity <20 mL/kg or decrease >30% compared to baseline in 24 h; also PaO2 <70 mmHg or PaCO2 >50 mmHg.
  • Other criteria: bulbar weakness (dysphagia, dysphonia, aspiration), severe dysautonomia (arrhythmias, labile hypertension, orthostatic hypotension, paralytic ileus), rapid progression (inability to ambulate in <1 week).
  • The forced vital capacity is measured serially (every 4-6 hours) in patients with suspected respiratory compromise; drop <20 mL/kg predicts need for mechanical ventilation in >80%.
  • Dysautonomia may require continuous monitoring of heart rate and blood pressure.
  • Non-invasive mechanical ventilation is not recommended; most require elective orotracheal intubation if forced vital capacity continues to decline.

items.title: Forecast
items.contentHtml:
Prognosis

  • The majority of patients (80%) achieve complete or almost complete functional recovery after one year; 20% present permanent motor or sensory sequelae.
  • Approximate mortality of 5%, mainly due to complications: cardiac arrest due to dysautonomia, aspiration pneumonia, sepsis, pulmonary thromboembolism.
  • Poor prognostic factors: age >60 years, need for mechanical ventilation, history of Campylobacter jejuni, axonal variant, rapid onset (<7 days), distal motor potential amplitude <20% of the lower limit.
  • Recovery usually begins 2-4 weeks after the nadir; the plateau may last days to weeks.
  • Early rehabilitation (physical therapy, occupational therapy) improves functional outcome.

items.foco: Contraindication of corticosteroids: no benefit, may worsen
items.title: Contraindication of corticosteroids
items.contentHtml:
Contraindication of corticosteroids

  • Corticosteroids (prednisone, methylprednisolone) are contraindicated in the treatment of Guillain-Barré syndrome because they do not demonstrate benefit and may worsen the prognosis.
  • Clinical studies (including meta-analysis) show that corticosteroids alone or in combination with IVIG or plasmapheresis do not improve recovery and may delay it.
  • Proposed mechanism: corticosteroids could inhibit remyelination and increase secondary axonal damage.
  • Exception: if there is an absolute contraindication for IVIG and plasmapheresis (rare), corticosteroids are not used as an alternative; Support and expectant management should be considered.
  • In chronic variants (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy) corticosteroids are effective, but not in the acute form.
